query: evidence on routine nasobiliary drainage after endoscopic clearance of CBD in choledocholithiasis-induced cholangitis

retrieved_docs.title: Choledocholithiasis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Cholecystectomy is not indicated for primary CBD stones. Other surgical options include open choledochotomy, transcystic exploration (a technique to clear the CBD of stones during laparoscopic cholecystectomy), percutaneous extraction, and extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy. The choice of treatment for choledocholithiasis found during surgery being done for cholelithiasis or cholecystitis includes intraoperative common bile duct exploration, intraoperative ERCP, and postoperative ERCP. The intraoperative procedure can be performed if consent was obtained preoperatively. Otherwise, ERCP is recommended at a later time, but during the same hospitalization.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Transhepatic Cholangiography -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tissue collection via biopsy, aspiration, or brushing

retrieved_docs.title: T-Tube -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: In patients diagnosed with both cholelithiasis and choledocholithiasis, CBD clearance accompanied by a synchronized or consequent cholecystectomy is recommended. Two well-established safe approaches are step-wise ERCP and consequent surgery, or synchronized open or laparoscopic approaches to the CBD exploration, clearance, and cholecystectomy. Current studies have not determined significant differences among the mentioned approaches. In patients who are decided to be managed with laparoscopic cholecystectomy, the presence of choledocholithiasis and the successful removal either with cystic duct approach or with a direct choledochotomy should be documented with contrast cholangiogram intraoperatively. Both mentioned approaches demand a set of specialized instrumentation, and in case of lacking the demanding equipment, a consequent ERCP would be scheduled. Following a direct choledochotomy, the size of the CBD should be evaluated. While primary repair might be considered as acceptable management in the large CBDs, the smaller sized choledochal duct should be repaired over an appropriate size T-tube. The T-tube size ranges from 10 to 18 Fr. However, the T-tube length might be customized according to the specific length of the CBD to be implemented. The CBD condition should be checked weeks following the T-tube placement, and in unsuccessful endoscopic choledocholithiasis removal, probably due to the impacted stones, transduodenal sphincterotomy might be recommended. In complete unsuccessful cases of choledocholithiasis disimpaction, a surgical bypass method, including choledochoduodenostomy or Roux-en-Y choledochojejunostomy, would be preferred.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Transhepatic Cholangiography -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Bile duct stone removal and lithotripsy [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Bile Duct Stricture -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Implement evidence-based treatment strategies, including endoscopic, percutaneous, and surgical options. Assess the risks and benefits of various management approaches based on individual patient factors. Differentiate between benign and malignant causes of biliary strictures using appropriate diagnostic tools. Communicate the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance care delivery for patients with bile duct strictures. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Obstruction -- Treatment / Management -- CBD Stricture
retrieved_docs.content: Benign Endoscopic sphincterotomy and balloon dilatation Endoscopic stenting with stent removal/exchange at 4 to 6 weeks Biliary-enteric bypass if and when surgery is required Malignant Endoscopic drainage with stenting Percutaneous drainage with PTC with internal and external drainage Palliative biliary-enteric bypass in unresectable cases Resection of the tumor with biliary-enteric anastomosis in resectable disease [20]

retrieved_docs.title: T-Tube -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Indications of T Tube can be summarized in one concept that is to stent and drain the common bile duct after choledochotomy [11] . The most common reason for performing choledochotomy is the extraction of biliary stones [2] . Choledochotomy, stone extraction, and T Tube placement was common procedure for intractable stones before the ERCP era. Since ERCP became available and expertise was developed, it became the common and less aggressive approach to CBD stones extractions [12] [4] . The number of choledochotomies became much less common and unfamiliar to many surgeons trained and practiced after 2000.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Cholangiography -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the indications for percutaneous cholangiography. Describe the technique of percutaneous cholangiography. Outline the appropriate evaluation of the potential complications and clinical significance of percutaneous cholangiography. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance percutaneous cholangiography and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Biliary Cholangitis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the factors associated with increased risk of developing primary biliary cholangitis. Assess patient history clues that might prompt consideration of primary biliary cholangitis. Evaluate the management options for patients affected by primary biliary cholangitis. Communicate the importance of coordination within the interprofessional team for rapid diagnosis and treatment of biliary cholangitis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cholangitis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management bundle includes the diagnostic tools highlighted in the recent update, the Tokyo 2018 guidelines, and includes additional diagnostic and prognostic elements. It also includes helpful timelines for favorable responses to initial treatment in patients with mild cases, as well as the performance of the biliary tract drainage in those with moderate and severe disease. There are also helpful criteria to identify those patients who may benefit from ICU-level care and patient transfer to a hospital capable of providing endoscopic or percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage. The bundle can be found in a simple checklist that is not only great for healthcare providers to follow but can help ensure the use of the bundle throughout the patients' hospital course.

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Pyogenic Cholangitis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Although rare, RPC can present acutely with symptoms in keeping with acute ascending cholangitis. When severe, patients can present in a life-threatening condition. Specific guidelines for managing acute RPC do not exist per se, but guidelines for the initial management of acute ascending cholangitis remain relevant, given the clinical overlap. Management bundles such as these facilitate a standard approach to the disease across sites, encourage optimized local protocols, and provide a framework within which a clinician can provide excellent patient care. The Tokyo Guidelines (2018) are a series of management bundles for acute cholangitis and cholecystitis. They include a helpful stepwise approach to managing acute cholangitis, taking into account the severity of presentation and providing guidance on when to consider drainage procedures and transfer to more specialist centers. Frameworks such as the Tokyo Guidelines can improve patient prognosis if used appropriately. Still, it is important to be aware that advanced management of pyogenic cholangitis does differ from acute ascending cholangitis, so these guidelines should be used with care and by appropriately trained clinicians. [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Cholelithiasis (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, tests such as endoscopic or magnetic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP/MRCP) are sometimes useful when working up patients with jaundice and dilated CBD or suspected cholangitis, but are usually obtained after an ultrasound. ERCP is an invasive test, requiring the use of contrast dye but also has the advantage of allowing intervention if pathology is found (e.g., stenting, stone extraction, biopsy).  MRCP, on the other hand, is non-invasive and does not require contrast dye.

retrieved_docs.title: Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography. Describe the technique involved in performing an endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography. Outline the potential complications of endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography. Explain interprofessional team strategies to optimize care coordination and communication and advance the appropriate and efficacious utilization of endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cholelithiasis (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with symptoms and workup consistent with acute cholecystitis will require admission to hospital, surgical consult and intravenous antibiotics.  Patients with choledocholithiasis or gallstone pancreatitis will also require admission to hospital, gastrointestinal (GI) consultation and ERCP or MRCP. Patients with acute ascending cholangitis are usually ill-appearing and septic. They often also require aggressive resuscitation and ICU-level care in addition to surgical intervention to drain an infection in the biliary tract. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Choledocholithiasis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Today in most experienced centers, ERCP can be performed with minimal morbidity and mortality. The rate of complications reported varies from 1-5%. The biggest problem with ERCP is the technical part, which can be difficult in about 5% of cases. Retained gallstones may occur in less than 1% of people, and pancreatitis is known to occur in less than 3% of patients. [15] [16] [17] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Cholecystitis -- Treatment / Management -- Nonsurgical Management Approaches
retrieved_docs.content: Severely ill or high-risk patients who cannot tolerate surgery may undergo percutaneous gallbladder drainage performed by interventional radiology. This intervention may serve as definitive therapy for elderly or comorbid patients or as a bridge to elective cholecystectomy within 4 to 8 weeks of drain placement. [23] In patients who decline surgery, interventional radiology can conduct outpatient tube studies to confirm cystic duct patency before drain removal. However, recurrence rates of acute cholecystitis after drain removal may reach up to 47%. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 878
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are performed to visualize, assess, and treat pathology within a variety of anatomic regions. Within this category, specific methods are defined, each tailored to the organ system or clinical scenario involved. Cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct is a recognized method, and it can be further specified as Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with cholangiography or as Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with choledochoenterostomy. A related biliary approach, Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or removal of calculus—performed with or without cholecystotomy and often accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty—may be included as part of an exploration procedure. Laparoscopic surgery also provides a method for biliary exploration, exemplified by Laparoscopy, surgical; cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct. Cranial

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2252
retrieved_docs.content: Cholecystectomy and common duct exploration is classified as a specific subprocedure within the broader surgical category of استئصال المرارة (gallbladder removal). In the context of استئصال المرارة, صورة الأوعية الصفراوية (imaging of the biliary ducts) is closely related, as this imaging modality is routinely used to assess the anatomy of the biliary tree, guide intra‑operative decision‑making, and ensure complete clearance of stones when a common duct exploration is performed. The integration of صورة الأوعية الصفراوية with cholecystectomy and common duct exploration highlights the importance of combining diagnostic imaging and therapeutic surgery to optimize patient outcomes and reduce postoperative complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2757
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures represent a broad category of surgical interventions in which direct visual or instrument‑guided examination of an anatomic region is combined with therapeutic action. In the biliary system, cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct can be performed as a stand‑alone procedure, as a laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, or as part of a more extensive operation that includes transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography. A related biliary exploration, choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, may also incorporate transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, indicating that these adjunctive techniques are possibly included within the primary exploration. Cranial exploration procedures include supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy performed for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes, as well as suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2757
retrieved_docs.content: or endocrine glands—often supplemented by adjunctive methods such as sphincterotomy, cholangiography, or mediastinal access. Recognizing these interrelated techniques aids clinicians in selecting appropriate operative plans, anticipating required ancillary steps, and optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1610
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic agents for bile duct patency testing and gastric secretion test diagnostic agents are both classified as “other diagnostic agents in ATC.” These agents are used for specialized diagnostic procedures: the former assess the openness of the bile ducts, while the latter evaluate gastric secretory function. By being grouped under the broader ATC category of other diagnostic agents, they share a common regulatory and therapeutic classification despite serving distinct clinical testing purposes.

A minimally invasive variant, “Laparoscopy, surgical; cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct,” also falls under this methodological group. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 743
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to investigate, access, and treat pathology in diverse anatomical regions, and they are organized under the parent clinical actions “Clinical actions A – G.” Within this category, a partial or complete adrenalectomy—or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy—can be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also include excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Cholecystectomy procedures that incorporate exploration of the common bile duct are represented in several variants: standard duct exploration, duct exploration with cholangiography, and duct exploration combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography; these can be executed laparoscopically. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 884
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are defined by the inclusion of a systematic examination or investigation of an anatomic region as part of the operative plan. Within this category, a variety of specific methods are recognized. Cholecystectomy with exploration of the common bile duct—performed either with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography)—is listed as a distinct exploration method, and laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common duct exploration is also classified under the same heading. Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, optionally combined with cholecystotomy and transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, is identified as a possibly included method of exploration. Cranial exploration procedures include exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad operative category in which direct visual or instrumental assessment of an anatomic region is performed, often together with therapeutic interventions, and are organized under the parent clinical‑action set “Clinical actions A‑G.” In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, dorsally, or via a lumbar approach, with or without biopsy—serves as an exploration procedure and may include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration comprises cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration and may be combined with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, followed by choledochotomy or choledochostomy for drainage, calculus removal, or cholecystotomy. retrieved_docs.content: Bile acids and derivatives for bile therapy are classified as bile therapy drugs, representing a specific category of medications used to manage and treat conditions affecting bile production, secretion, or flow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1166
retrieved_docs.content: CPTAC Procedures Codelist is a collection of surgical procedure concepts that includes the cholecystectomy (استئصال المرارة) and the intra‑operative cholangiogram. The procedure described as “cholecystectomy and common duct exploration” is classified as a subcategory of cholecystectomy, indicating that it expands the basic gallbladder removal to also address the common bile duct. Cholecystectomy is linked to the imaging study known as cholangiogram (صورة الأوعية الصفراوية), which visualizes the biliary tree. The intra‑operative cholangiogram is a specific type of cholangiogram performed during surgery and therefore is a child concept of the general cholangiogram. These relationships show how the CPTAC list organizes related biliary surgical procedures and their associated imaging modalities, supporting consistent coding and documentation in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2662
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic agents for bile duct patency testing and gastric secretion test diagnostic agents are both classified as subcategories of the broader ATC group “other diagnostic agents in ATC.” This ATC group serves as a collective category for various diagnostic substances, and it includes specific agents such as urea C‑13. By grouping these specialized diagnostic agents under “other diagnostic agents in ATC,” the classification system provides a structured framework that links procedures for assessing bile duct patency and gastric secretion to related diagnostic tools, facilitating organized reference, selection, and use of agents like urea C‑13 in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad surgical category that encompasses organ‑specific and region‑specific operative techniques used to investigate, diagnose, and treat pathology across multiple anatomical sites. Within this category, adrenalectomy (partial or total) may be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches and can include biopsy or excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Cholecystectomy combined with common‑bile‑duct exploration includes standard duct exploration, cholangiography‑guided exploration, choledochoenterostomy, and transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography; similar principles apply to choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that incorporate exploration, drainage, or calculus removal. Cranial exploration is represented by exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy, subdivided into infratentorial (posterior‑fossa) and supratentorial approaches, as well as suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1219
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures are defined as a broad group of surgical actions (Clinical actions A–G) used to investigate, decompress, or remove pathology across multiple anatomic regions, and each named technique functions either as a primary “method” or as a “possibly‑included method” when combined with adjunctive steps. In the adrenal gland, partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and when coupled with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor, are classified as exploration methods. Biliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration performed with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography), as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy with drainage, calculus removal, and optional sphincterotomy; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is recorded similarly.
